questions.question: Describe the contributions of Shams-ud-din Iltutmish to the Delhi Sultanate. Why is he called the real founder?
questions.answer: Shams-ud-din Iltutmish, who ruled from 1211 to 1236, is considered the real founder of the Delhi Sultanate because he transformed a fragile kingdom into a stable empire. He was Aibak's slave and son-in-law, and had been the Governor of Badaun before overthrowing Aibak's weak son Aram Shah. His first major contribution was shifting the capital from Lahore to Delhi, making it the permanent seat of Muslim power in India. He faced numerous revolts from rival nobles and governors but dealt with them strongly, consolidating his empire to include the Indus, Multan, Sindh, and Bengal. He introduced the iqta system, where land was assigned to officers who had to maintain troops in return, which became the backbone of Sultanate administration. He was the first Muslim king to introduce the silver tanka and copper jital coins, establishing a proper monetary system. He completed the construction of both the Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque and the Qutub Minar that Aibak had started. He maintained a Standing Army which strengthened the Sultanate militarily. He also showed great diplomatic wisdom in 1221 by refusing shelter to the ruler of Persia who was being chased by Genghis Khan, thereby preventing a devastating Mongol invasion of India. His creation of the Chahalgani, a group of forty faithful slaves, provided him with loyal administrators.

questions.question: Discuss the administrative, military, and economic reforms of Ala-ud-din Khalji.
questions.answer: Ala-ud-din Khalji, who ruled from 1296 to 1316, was the greatest ruler of the Khalji dynasty and implemented sweeping reforms across administration, military, and economy. In his economic reforms, he abolished the system of small iqtas and converted them into Crown lands called Khalsa under direct state control. He was the first Sultan to order the measurement of cultivable land, fixing revenue at one-half of the produce for the fertile Ganga-Yamuna Doab and one-third for other areas. His market regulations were revolutionary: he established three separate markets in Delhi for food grains, cloth, and horses/slaves/cattle. Each market had a superintendent called Shahna-i-Mandi, supported by intelligence officers called barids and secret spies called munhiyans who monitored compliance. Violators were severely punished or imprisoned. In military reforms, he was the first ruler of Delhi to maintain a permanent standing army with soldiers paid in cash. He introduced Dagh, the branding of horses to prevent fraud, and Huliya or Chehra, the system of maintaining descriptive rolls of soldiers. These reforms allowed him to maintain a large and efficient army at low cost. His military conquests were equally impressive as he sent Malik Kafur south to defeat the Yadavas, Kakatiyas, Hoysalas, and Pandyas, becoming the first Muslim ruler to extend the empire to the extreme south. He was also a patron of arts with Amir Khusrau as his court poet, and built the Alai Darwaza, Siri Fort, and many maktabs and madrasas.

questions.question: Explain the various schemes of Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq and why they failed.
questions.answer: Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq, who ruled from 1325 to 1351, was the most learned scholar of his time, versed in Arabic, Persian, philosophy, astronomy, logic, and mathematics. However, his ambitious schemes consistently failed due to poor execution and lack of political judgement. His first major scheme was the excessive taxation in the Doab region, where he raised land revenue twenty-fold. Unfortunately, this coincided with a famine, and the desperate peasants revolted. He was forced to withdraw the harsh tax orders. His second scheme was the transfer of capital from Delhi to Devagiri, renamed Daulatabad, in 1327. While strategically sound since Daulatabad was more centrally located, the execution was disastrous. Only the upper classes were forced to shift, the harsh journey caused immense suffering and many deaths, and he could not control Delhi from the distant southern location, forcing a humiliating return to Delhi in 1335. His third major failure was the introduction of token currency, where he replaced the silver tanka with copper jittal. Without proper security measures, people easily forged the cheap copper coins at home, flooding the market and collapsing the currency. He was forced to withdraw the token currency at great financial loss. His fourth failure was the Khurasan and Qurachil military expeditions, where the army received one year's salary in advance, only for the campaigns to be abandoned. These repeated failures lost him the trust of nobles and ulema, leading to widespread revolts, with Bengal becoming independent and the Vijayanagara kingdom (1336) and Bahmani kingdom (1345) being founded. He died in 1351 while fighting a rebellion in Sindh.

questions.question: Why is Firoz Shah Tughlaq called a reformer? Describe his reforms in detail.
questions.answer: Firoz Shah Tughlaq, who ruled from 1351 to 1388, earned the title of reformer through his wide-ranging beneficial changes across every area of governance. In economic reforms, he abolished the harsh and arbitrary taxes imposed by earlier rulers and established a fair system of four main taxes: Kharaj (land tax of one-tenth), Zakat (Muslim religious tax), Kham (one-fifth of war booty), and Jizya (tax on non-Muslims). He also collected irrigation tax, garden tax, octroi, and sales tax, but his overall approach was far more humane than his predecessors. His public works were remarkable: he built canals from the Yamuna to Hissar and from the Sutlej to the Ghaggar to improve irrigation and agriculture. He built hospitals for the sick, constructed new towns including Jaunpur, Firozpur, Firozabad, and Hissar-Firoza, and built sarais (rest houses) for travellers. He repaired the Qutub Minar which had been damaged. In judicial reforms, he replaced the excessive and cruel punishments common in earlier reigns with petty fines and short-term punishments, making justice more humane. In education, he had Sanskrit books translated into Persian to promote cross-cultural learning, and built schools and colleges throughout his kingdom. His welfare measures included the establishment of the Diwan-i-Khairat for the poor, an Employment Bureau to help people find work, and financial assistance for marriages of those who could not afford them. However, his decision to make iqtas hereditary ultimately weakened central authority and contributed to the empire's decline after his death in 1388.

questions.question: Trace the rise and fall of the Slave Dynasty from 1206 to 1290.
questions.answer: The Slave Dynasty, also called the Mamluk Dynasty (Mamluk means slave in Arabic), ruled from 1206 to 1290 and was the first dynasty of the Delhi Sultanate. It began with Qutb-ud-din Aibak, a slave of Muhammad Ghori who laid the foundation of the Sultanate in 1206 after the decisive Second Battle of Tarain in 1192. Aibak was crowned in Lahore, earned the title Lakh Baksh for his generosity, and began construction of the Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque and Qutub Minar before dying in 1210 while playing polo. His son Aram Shah proved weak and was overthrown by Iltutmish, Aibak's slave and son-in-law. Iltutmish (1211-1236) is considered the real founder of the Delhi Sultanate, shifting the capital to Delhi, introducing the iqta system, silver tanka, and copper jital coins, and completing the Qutub Minar. He wisely avoided a Mongol invasion in 1221 by refusing shelter to the ruler of Persia. After his death, his daughter Raziya Sultan (1236-1240) became the first and last Muslim woman to rule Delhi, but was killed by nobles who could not accept a woman ruler. A period of weak rulers followed until Ghiyas-ud-din Balban (1266-1286) restored order through his policy of blood and iron. He crushed the Mewatis, introduced Zaminbosi/Sijda, propagated Divine Rights, and created the Diwan-i-Arz. After Balban's death in 1286, his grandson proved too weak to hold power, and in 1290, the Khaljis captured the throne, ending the 84-year reign of the Slave Dynasty.

questions.question: Why did the Delhi Sultanate disintegrate after the Tughlaqs? Discuss the Sayyid and Lodi dynasties.
questions.answer: The Delhi Sultanate disintegrated after the Tughlaqs due to a combination of internal weaknesses and external invasion. Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq's failed schemes had severely weakened the empire, with many provinces revolting and becoming independent, including the emergence of the Vijayanagara kingdom in 1336 and the Bahmani kingdom in 1345. Firoz Shah Tughlaq's decision to make iqtas hereditary gave nobles too much power and weakened central authority. After his death in 1388, weak successors could not maintain unity. The devastating invasion of Timur in 1398 dealt the final blow, leaving the Sultanate crumbling and confined to a small territory by 1412. The Sayyid Dynasty (1414-1451) was founded by Khizr Khan, Timur's viceroy, who took the title Rayati-Ala. His successors Mubarak Shah, Muhammad Shah Sayyid, and Ala-ud-din Alam Shah were progressively weaker. The last ruler Alam Shah was so incompetent that his own wazir Hamid Khan invited Bahlul Lodi to take power, and Alam Shah left for Badaun. The Lodi Dynasty (1451-1526) brought Afghan rulers to the throne. Bahlul Lodi suppressed revolts and defeated the Sultan of Jaunpur. His son Sikandar Lodi was the greatest Lodi ruler, moving the capital to Agra and implementing welfare measures, though he re-imposed jizya. The last ruler Ibrahim Lodi faced constant revolts, and his own uncle Daulat Khan Lodi invited Babur from Kabul. Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the Battle of Panipat in 1526, ending 320 years of Sultanate rule.

questions.question: Compare the Khalji and Tughlaq dynasties in terms of their rulers, expansion, and administration.
questions.answer: The Khalji Dynasty (1290-1320) and the Tughlaq Dynasty (1320-1414) were the two most significant dynasties of the Delhi Sultanate, each with distinct characteristics. The Khaljis were Central Asian Turkic people who had settled in Afghanistan and ruled for three decades. Their greatest ruler was Ala-ud-din Khalji, who expanded the empire to the extreme south through Malik Kafur's campaigns against the Yadavas, Kakatiyas, Hoysalas, and Pandyas. In administration, Ala-ud-din was known for strict market regulations with three markets supervised by the Shahna-i-Mandi, and his economic reforms abolished small iqtas in favour of Crown lands. His military reforms including Dagh and Huliya created a disciplined standing army. The Tughlaqs had a longer reign of nearly a century. Their founder Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq was half-Indian and half-Turkish. Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq was the most learned scholar but lacked political judgement, and his failed schemes like the transfer of capital to Daulatabad and token currency weakened the empire. Firoz Shah Tughlaq was the reformer who abolished harsh taxes, built canals, hospitals, and new towns, and promoted education. While the Khalji administration was characterized by strict control and harsh measures, the Tughlaq administration under Firoz Shah was more welfare-oriented. However, the Khaljis maintained stronger central authority, while Firoz Shah's decision to make iqtas hereditary ultimately weakened the Tughlaq empire beyond recovery.

questions.question: Describe the measures taken by Ala-ud-din Khalji to maintain a large army at low cost and control his empire effectively.
questions.answer: Ala-ud-din Khalji devised an interconnected system of economic, market, and military reforms that allowed him to maintain a large standing army at low cost while keeping strict control over his vast empire. His economic reforms started with abolishing small iqtas and converting them into Crown lands called Khalsa under direct state control, which increased government revenue. He was the first Sultan to order the measurement of cultivable land, fixing revenue at one-half of the produce for the Ganga-Yamuna Doab and one-third for other areas. These harsh revenue measures, while oppressive to peasants, ensured a steady flow of funds to the treasury. His market regulations were designed specifically to keep prices low so that soldiers could be paid less while still maintaining a good standard of living. He established three markets in Delhi for food grains, cloth, and horses with strict price controls enforced by the Shahna-i-Mandi, barids (intelligence officers), and munhiyans (secret spies). Violators faced severe punishment. In military reforms, he was the first ruler to keep a permanent standing army paid in cash rather than through land grants. He introduced Dagh (branding of horses) to prevent commanders from presenting the same horses at multiple inspections, and Huliya or Chehra (descriptive rolls of soldiers) to prevent ghost soldiers on the payroll. Through this comprehensive system, Ala-ud-din maintained an army powerful enough to conquer Gujarat, Rajasthan, Ranthambore, and Chittor in the north, and through Malik Kafur, defeat the southern kingdoms, making him master of north India by 1306.
